speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 18th of February, 1996. And we are in Sydney, Australia. And camera is ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sarah, can you tell me about any other experiences you had in Krasnodar?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sitting in a big garden, where the child, she was three months old. And I didn't have with what to feed her. So I went to the hospital to give, and she got dysentery, because I pushed her food in mouth. I put a piece of bread in a piece of material and give her to suck it. I put a bit of tomato, I gave her. And then she got dysentery. I took her to the hospital. And I left her there, and we started to run. We went to Baku.

speaker: interviewer
text: You left her there.

speaker: interviewee
text: At hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have to go. They pushed us out. The Germans came. And I went from there. I left the girl there, and I went to Kazakhstan. To the army, not to Kazakhstan. To the army. We enroll ourselves in the Polish Anders' Army. The Army Anders.

speaker: interviewer
text: The underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anders. The name of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the army unit was Anders.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you enlist? In Krasnodar?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Baku.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went across to a place called Baku.

speaker: interviewee
text: Baku.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was Baku from Krasnodar approximately? You don't remember. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there, we enrolled in the Polish army, and we went to where I told you. Novosibirsk or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, Novosibirsk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, when we came, the top, we went walking, and the top and the bottom were standing Poles who was in the army. The Jewish religion came already. [NON-ENGLISH] And then we were there a few nights sleeping upon bridge. And if one want to turn, all 20 have to turn around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were so close together.

speaker: interviewee
text: So close. Tight. Man and woman. And from there, they took us. We scheduled the trains to Krasnodar. To not Krasnodar. Kazakhstan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kazakhstan. From Novosibirsk, you went to Kazakhstan.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there they took us to a  village. They call it a kolkhoz. And there we've been sleeping on the floor. They all been sleeping on the floor. Near the stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: To keep you warm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not even keep us warm. But the pieces what I robbed a girl. We covered ourself. We didn't have a blanket. It was very cold. And then the people put together money. They bought us a blanket.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which people?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was Polish people. A lot of immigrants.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they all clubbed together, and they bought a blanket. For whom did they buy the blanket?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me and my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And what did you say? You robbed the girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: What you put for a child to make it not to make wet?

speaker: interviewer
text: A napkin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Napkins. The napkins what we have. We cover ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see. And what happened to your little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: My little girl died. She got dysentery.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did she die? Before you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I left. In Krasnodar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she died in Krasnodar in the hospital before you left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a funeral for her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I didn't ever know when she died. I wrote to them, and they said she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, when you left, she was still alive. When you had to flee from the Germans--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was still alive, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you wrote to the hospital, and they said that she didn't live. I see. OK. So, now you came to-- what was the last place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kazakhstan.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kazakhstan, we've been quite a few years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were there with the Polish army?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We left the Polish army. And we went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do when you were in the Polish army? What did you hope to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to the border. They took to the border to go to England and to Israel. A lot went to Israel and to England, but we left. We didn't have what to eat. We've been starving from cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Kazakhstan.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the way was the army from Kazakhstan.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you joined the army, it was of no benefit to you, really.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Was only the hope that we would go overseas.

speaker: interviewer
text: So joining the army meant that you just had a train that took you from one place to another.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they didn't feed you.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they feed in rationing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they didn't give you warm clothes or blankets or anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. No. My husband had diarrhea. I have to go out and make a hole in the ice to wash it. His pants.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the train, yes. When the train stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And then, when you came to Kazakhstan, you left the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: I left the army, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your husband doing for the army? Or didn't he have to do anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. My husband, he was 19 years old. What can he do? He just finished high school when the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came to Kazakhstan, some people remained there hoping to get to England or to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Or to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Palestine. And you, what did you do when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We were working in the field.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed there for how long did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there for a few years till I found my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did some of those people who were traveling with you-- did they manage to get to Palestine at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And some did manage to get to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. I didn't have any contact with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Couldn't you get anywhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: We been always lice and dirty. We went out from the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live there in Kazakhstan?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Together with the village people. With a family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somebody gave you a room.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a room. Together with them in one home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you slept in one room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five. The couple with a son. And my husband and me. I think the son, they took him to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you eat, Sarah? How did you look after yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] How do you say [NON-ENGLISH]? The manager from the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: The mayor of the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the mayor. Give us rationing. We have to go to beg for something to eat. He gave us a sweet corn. And we have two breaks with a handle. We put it in and turn it around and make flour and bake.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have cooking facilities in that house? Or baking facilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. She has a big bowl. Like oven in the bottom. It was to make fire. And that's what's cooking for the whole lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in those a couple of years that you were there, did either of you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not at all. We have one pair of shoes and one overcoat, my husband's overcoat. When he went out, I went inside. But when he went out-- when I went out, he was inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you wore his shoes and his coat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you not get work of any kind there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't give us work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was this family who took you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Kazakh. Kazakhs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not Jewish family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not Jewish family.

speaker: interviewer
text: They just took you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did what their [NON-ENGLISH]. How do you say [NON-ENGLISH]? The mayor or whatever. He gave us places.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they pay them for taking you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was very kind of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were hunkered. It was a kettle around. They had been sitting. The Kazakh was-- how was the meal there? Was sitting all around him. He was in the middle. And if they took out the pasta, he was cutting the pasta in small pieces. And he gave everybody a bit with the hand like that. And then the juice from the meat was one little bowl, and everybody drink from the same bowl a sip.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the meal. So how did you survive in those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hard. Very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were obviously there over the winters and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, terrible. Winter of '42 was the worst winter of the lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: With no shoes. What was the floors of the house made of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just sand floors.

speaker: interviewee
text: Clay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a clay.

speaker: interviewee
text: Clay. And straw.

speaker: interviewer
text: You slept on straw. And you walked on the clay, and you had no shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was no warmth in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. There was a cat. And the cat was going around and saying meow to give him to eat. And my husband says, Sarah, meow. I said, meow, meow.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Oh, dear. And they managed to find enough food for the cat as well? Oh, they've been all right. They have cattle. They have lamb. The main meal-- the main thing was lamb. Once, the mayor gave us a piece of meat. We cooked the meat in the big-- how do you say-- A pot.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not a pot. They put it in a big round casserole.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the woman, she said, I saw that the horse died in the road, and they gave you that meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the mayor of the town brought this meat to your house from a horse that died.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what she said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's what she said. Did you believe here?

speaker: interviewee
text: We ate it anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were so hungry. Sarah, what about your clothes? Were you still wearing the same clothes you got from the relatives in Kiev?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a dress and strips. And then the [NON-ENGLISH], the manager from the village gave me a sack from sugar. And I made myself a dress. And I can sew like a queen going with the dress.

speaker: interviewer
text: From a sugar bag. You sewed it yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Everybody envied me. And then I find boots from the army and made shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You made shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I made sew it around. Yes. I made with my hands. And always I weared it, or he weared it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm. And what about-- your husband's clothes must've also been torn and broken.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you just repaired it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would repair and repair it and repair it. I didn't have cotton to repair.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you got up every morning, you sat around this house.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember any details.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was outside in the streets? Were the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wasn't a street. Wasn't a street. The Germans wasn't in Kazakhstan. The Germans was in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was outside? The Polish army? The Russian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not army. The villagers.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: The people from the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a farming village?

speaker: interviewee
text: A farm. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's all. And you remained there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remained there for quite a few years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one night I wake, and I said to my husband, give me a pencil. And I remembered the address for my brother in Israel. And I wrote a letter. And the Red Cross found us. And my brother sent me a parcel. The Red Cross, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know from where the Red Cross. But I didn't get it. The people from the village steal it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know about the parcel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Came from the postman. Came a letter that I send you a parcel. But I didn't get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you then, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. That's what I remember. My husband had dysentery. He went out behind the house to make his business, and the doctor was standing at the top straightaway. I have to go kilometers to fetch water.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no running water.

speaker: interviewee
text: No running water.

speaker: interviewer
text: And no toilet in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: No toilet. What you talking? It was so primitive like Stone Age. We make flour with stones to turn around to make flour from the corn, from the sweet corn.

speaker: interviewer
text: You dried the corn first.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was dried. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did all this come to an end? How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, my brother--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister find. She was in Jambul.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one that you parted with in Kiev.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was in-- Jambul belongs to Uzbekistan. She wrote me a letter, and we went there to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she know where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see. Had she been in Jambul all the years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: She went straight from Kiev to Jambul.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know how she got there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she wrote to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: She wrote to me, and we went there. And she was quite a good life, because he was manager in a torf. You know what torf? Torf is like coal, but they dig it out. Soft one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. In a mine. In a mine.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not a mine. It's in a field.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: They dig it out and throw it out. And it was dry, and you can burn it like coal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was managing a place like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the plant, they gave 600 grams of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave the 600 grams?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people where they were living in Jambul.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jambul. That was a village, also. And if you extend inside to cut the torf out, you get more bread. I had been standing there, and my feet swell. And my husband-- from the water.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were standing in the water.

speaker: interviewee
text: To get more bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were standing in the water in the hole that they dug out so that you could get more torf.

speaker: interviewee
text: More bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for each extra that you dug out, you got more bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. People who were standing in the water digging the torf got more bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your husband do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was managing.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was managing with your brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My brother-in-law went to the army, and he took his post.

speaker: interviewer
text: His place. And you were working as well digging the torf.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And we were sleeping together in one room. My brother-in-law and my sister and the two children and me and my husband. And in the same room, my sister had a lamb. They give her a lamb. And they didn't want to leave it outside, so the lamb was in the same room because people steal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the lamb was in the room all day.

speaker: interviewee
text: The night only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only the night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the day was tied to the outside with a string.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a small room or a big room?

speaker: interviewee
text: In one end was my sister and the two children. [NON-ENGLISH] And in the other end, me and my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sleep on the bed or on the floor?

speaker: interviewee
text: This time, we sleep on the bed. It's not a bed made from wood or something. Made in bricks made higher.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And the sheep was in the room with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you live in Jambul?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jambul. Only for very long.

speaker: interviewer
text: A few years.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few years. And then we went to town. Not in Jambul. That was in a village. Then we went to Jambul to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Into the center of the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: In town. Jambul was a town. Is a town still now. And there my husband got a very big in health department. A very big job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Russians know that you were Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't try to hide your identity at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was so many Russians and so many all kinds of nationalities.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there a lot of Jewish people with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they didn't discriminate against the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not that I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they treated you quite well? I mean, they didn't treat you worse than anybody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They didn't. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the mayor, for example, of the town that you lived in, he was a Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: I presume so. I don't remember. But my husband was working. He got very big job there for the Department of Health. And he went from medical center, hospitals, and he write what they need.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they gave him-- how do you say-- something to write more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, at this time, we've been already well off. Well off.

speaker: interviewee
text: Better.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have your own place at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't have any other children at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had food to eat and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because they gave him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the time you spent in Jambul better than you had had up till then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. From Jambul, they took us to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Back to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the ended and you remained in Jambul all that time, was there any Jewish life in Jambul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was the Polish life.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no shul.

speaker: interviewee
text: No shul. No. Who said about shul? You know that Russia hasn't been religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had no Jewish life.

speaker: interviewee
text: No Jewish life whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: The years that you were moving.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. No Jewish life. Only Polish. We have a Polish school. The Anders made a [INAUDIBLE] there. And they sent parcels. And I got a pair of shoes. I forgot to tell you a nice story. The Polish army gave my husband a pair of shoes. And he went from the village to a little town to buy something or to sell something. I don't remember. And when he went back, he doesn't want to owe the new shoes, so he put it on his shoulder. And one-- not a cow. A male.

speaker: interviewer
text: A bull.

speaker: interviewee
text: A bull came and want to kick him. And the shoes fell on his horn. And he start to run, and my husband went after him. And then, when he put his head, the shoes fell down. Then he took the shoes back.

speaker: interviewer
text: [CHUCKLES] This was in the town that you were moving around. And you were in Jambul already, or not yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We've been in Kazakhstan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, Sarah, when your husband was in Jambul and he had this good job, what were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Ah, I had been sewing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You took in sewing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the people of the village.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who comes. This was a town. Jambul was a town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you sew? From new? Or did you repair? Or what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I sew-- Listen. What I need to sew. I don't remember. I remember only once I made a bra for a lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: All by hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: All by hand, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had no machine or anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I had a hand machine. I think I had a hand machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you acquire-- in the town that you lived in, Jambul, did you acquire any furniture or a table?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a table. Actually, we-- the home belongs to that friend of my husband. He was like brothers together. They finished high school. He was a teacher in high school. And they gave him the home. One home. And we lived together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He was single.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shlomo Garbash.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shlomo Garbash. Did he survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: He survived the war with us together. We went to Poland together. And he married in a kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after he went to Poland, he went to Palestine, and he married there.

speaker: interviewee
text: He married in kibbutz in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, on a Polish kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a Polish. This was an Israeli kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I understand, They had kibbutz in for Jewish youth movements.

speaker: interviewee
text: To who want to migrate to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. That's interesting. Is there anything else you remember, any incidents that you remember particularly from your travels across Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only one. The Germans were flying after us to shoot us. And we were hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a frightening experience for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I was like that. I was nine months pregnant. Eight months, actually. And the little children were screaming and hiding in the wheat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you have to stay there in that wheat field?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were running. We didn't stay there. We were running.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any good experiences that happened during those travels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good? Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not anything. Anybody who was kind to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was in the same position what we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was nobody that you met up with on the way that you traveled with and stayed together with?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only in kibbutz. When we came back from the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: But during your travels, it was just the two if you.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband lost his friend, the Garbash. We lost in Lutsk. And then we lost each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you met up with him again where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Back when you come back to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And, so, after Jambul, what did you do? How did you get back? You said you went back to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: They send us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Why did they send you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They freed Poland. And they send us. The Polish Anders Army sent us back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Jambul?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Jambul.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: By cattle train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cattle tracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: 21 Track.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And how long was the journey? Do you remember, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite a few weeks, because we stopped in every station to take water, to go to the toilet somewhere behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they always waited for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not for me. The whole lot run out. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And they waited for everybody to come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And once, my husband wasn't there, and the train start to go. And then my husband want to go run after the train. And they caught him by the hand to pull him up. And we thought the train would cut his legs, but they didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: If he'd been a minute later, he would have missed the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did the train take you to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: To where? To which place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember the name. Near the Russian-- near the German border. That belongs to Germany, and then the Poles took it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland, but the German-occupied part of Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And you remained there for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: We been quite a long time. There was a kibbutz. We went to the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remained there for a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: Till they took us to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the end of tape 2 of the interview with Sarah Brodaty. It's the 18th of February, 1996, and we are in Sydney, Australia.
